title: Comprehensive Guide Regression R INFS3822
body:
INFS3822 Comprehensive Guide in R including all steps required to build ML Regression Models

This guide summarises all the steps you learned during weeks 3 and 4 including data loading, preprocessing (data cleaning and transformation including the conversion of categorical variables into dummy variables), feature selection (using Pair Plot and Correlation Matrix Heatmap), splitting data into training and testing datasets, model training and testing to assess the performance of the models. This guide including the steps requires for building ML models for regression problems using linear regression, decision tree and neural network in R. First, upload the file data_online_retail_customers.csv into this notebook on Google Colab. Once the dataset is uploaded, you can conduct the following steps.

1 Loading Libraries The following libraries are the ones used during weeks 3 and 4 that cover the required libraries for logistic regression, decision tree, and neural networks. # install all R packages needed install . packages ( " gridExtra " ) install . packages ( " dplyr " ) install . packages ( " corrplot " ) install . packages ( " rpart . plot " ) install . packages ( " keras " ) install . packages ( " DALEX " )

library ( ggplot2 ) library ( dplyr ) library ( gridExtra ) library ( corrplot ) library ( rpart ) library ( rpart . plot ) library ( keras ) library ( DALEX )

2 Loading the Data from a CSV File # Load the data data <- read . csv ( " data _ online _ retail _ customers . csv " )

3 Data Cleaning # Check for missing values missing _ values <- is . na ( data ) print ( sum ( missing _ values ) ) [1] 0 # Remove rows with missing values , if any data <- na . omit ( data )

4 Data Transformation # Check the data type of all variables summary ( data )

Customer_ID Age Gender Annual_Income Min. : 1.0 Min. :18.00 Length:676 Min. : 20.01 1st Qu.:169.8 1st Qu.:31.00 Class :character 1st Qu.: 70.94 Median :338.5 Median :45.00 Mode :character Median :117.86 Mean :338.5 Mean :44.21 Mean :114.81 3rd Qu.:507.2 3rd Qu.:57.00 3rd Qu.:162.44 Max. :676.0 Max. :69.00 Max. :199.73 Total_Spend Years_as_Customer Num_of_Purchases Num_of_Returns Min. : 150 Min. : 1.000 Min. : 1.00 Min. :0.000 1st Qu.: 2819 1st Qu.: 5.000 1st Qu.:25.00 1st Qu.:2.000 Median : 4886 Median : 9.000 Median :49.00 Median :5.000 Mean : 5093 Mean : 9.722 Mean :50.17 Mean :4.657 3rd Qu.: 7609 3rd Qu.:14.000 3rd Qu.:76.00 3rd Qu.:7.000 Max. :10000 Max. :19.000 Max. :99.00 Max. :9.000 Num_of_Support_Contacts Satisfaction_Score Last_Purchase_Days_Ago Min. :0.000 Min. :1.000 Min. : 1.00 1st Qu.:1.000 1st Qu.:2.000 1st Qu.: 94.75 Median :2.000 Median :3.000 Median :176.00 Mean :1.945 Mean :3.013 Mean :183.31 3rd Qu.:3.000 3rd Qu.:4.000 3rd Qu.:273.00 Max. :4.000 Max. :5.000 Max. :364.00 Email_Opt_In Promotion_Response Churned Mode :logical Length:676 Mode :logical FALSE:326 Class :character FALSE:328 TRUE :350 Mode :character TRUE :348

# Convert categorical columns ( Class : character ) into ' factor ' type ( i . e . category ) data $ Gender = as . factor ( data $ Gender ) data $ Promotion _ Response = as . factor ( data $ Promotion _ Response )

Further data transformations Uncomment the lines you need by removing the # Modify the code to fit your needs # In this dataset , the target variable is Churned , and it is of type ' logical ' ( i . e . boolean ) . # If the target variable is binary ( e . g . Yes / No , Good / Bad , Success / Fail ) , make sure you convert it into boolean variable as follows : # data $ Churned = data $ Churned == 'Yes '

# Convert date columns into datetime based on your objectives . # For further details , refer to Week4 _ Pre _ Seminar _ Using _ R _ for _ Clustering # data [ ' vdate '] = pd . to _ datetime ( data [ ' vdate ']) # for dates in the format m / d / yyyy # data [ ' vdate '] = pd . to _ datetime ( data [ ' vdate '] , dayfirst = True ) # for dates in the format d / m / yyyy

# data $ vdate <- as . Date ( data $ vdate , format = "% m / % d / % Y ") # for dates in the format m / d / yyyy # data $ vdate <- as . Date ( data $ vdate , format = "% d / % m / % Y ") # for dates in the format d / m / yyyy

# If you have a variable representing a date , you can transform it into a categorical variable ( Think of what would be the categories that are relevant to your analysis objectives ) # For further details , refer to Week4 _ Pre _ Seminar _ Using _ R _ for _ Clustering # Create a categorical variable for seasons # get _ season <- function ( date ) { # month <- month ( date ) # if ( month % in % c (12 , 1 , 2) ) { # return ( ' Winter ') # } else if ( month % in % c (3 , 4 , 5) ) { # return ( ' Spring ') # } else if ( month % in % c (6 , 7 , 8) ) { # return ( ' Summer ') # } else { # return ( ' Autumn ') # } # } #
# data $ season <- sapply ( data $ vdate , get _ season ) # data $ season <- as . factor ( data $ season )

# You can transform some data values . For example , convert '5+ ' to numeric 5 in the ' rcount ' column ( Week4 _ Pre _ Seminar _ Using _ R _ for _ Clustering ) # data [ which ( data $ rcount == "5+") ,] $ rcount = "5" # data $ rcount <- as . integer ( data $ rcount )

# Scale the numeric input data # Identify numeric columns numeric _ columns <- sapply ( data , is . numeric )

# Scale numeric columns data [ , numeric _ columns ] <- scale ( data [ , numeric _ columns ])

5 Feature Selection # Split the data into inputs ( X as features ) and outputs ( y as target variable ) . # Initial feature selection ( i . e . selecting which variables should be included in X ) is required here as follows . # You can exclude variables that represent unique identifiers because they don ' t have any impact on the target variable . # You can exclude variables that are text descriptions and not useful as categories . # You can exclude categorical variables that have too many possible values , as these would generate too many dummy variables . A model with too many variables cannot perform well and sometimes cause the machine to run out of memory . This would also help you focus on the most important variables . X <- data _ selected <- data [ , ! ( names ( data ) % in % c ( " Customer _ ID " , " Total _ Spend " ) ) ] y <- data _ selected <- data $ Total _ Spend

Pair Plot and Correlation Matrix Heatmap # Determine the type of the target variable target _ is _ numeric <- is . numeric ( y ) target _ is _ categorical <- is . factor ( y ) target _ is _ logical <- is . logical ( y )

target <- " Total _ Spend "

# Plot each feature against the target variable using appropriate plots in a grid num _ features <- ncol ( X ) num _ cols <- 3 num _ rows <- ceiling ( num _ features / num _ cols ) # Ceiling division

# Create an empty list to store plots plots <- list ()

# Plot each feature against the target variable for ( i in seq _ len ( num _ features ) ) { feature <- colnames ( X ) [ i ] feature _ is _ numeric <- is . numeric ( X [[ feature ]]) feature _ is _ categorical <- is . factor ( X [[ feature ]])

# Select appropriate plot if (( target _ is _ categorical || target _ is _ logical ) & & feature _ is _ numeric ) { p <- ggplot ( data . frame (X , y ) , aes _ string ( x = " y " , y = feature ) ) + geom _ boxplot () + labs ( title = paste ( " Box Plot of " , feature , " by " , target ) , x = target , y = feature ) } else if ( target _ is _ numeric & & feature _ is _ numeric ) { p <- ggplot ( data . frame (X , y ) , aes _ string ( x = feature , y = " y " ) ) + geom _ point () + labs ( title = paste ( " Scatter Plot of " , feature , " vs " , target ) , x = feature , y = target ) } else if (( target _ is _ categorical || target _ is _ logical ) & & feature _ is _ categorical ) { p <- ggplot ( data . frame (X , y ) , aes _ string ( x = feature , fill = " y " ) ) + geom _ bar ( position = " dodge " ) + labs ( title = paste ( " Count Plot of " , feature , " by " , target ) , x = feature , fill = target ) } else if ( target _ is _ numeric & & feature _ is _ categorical ) {

p <- ggplot ( data . frame (X , y ) , aes _ string ( x = feature , y = " y " ) ) + geom _ boxplot () + labs ( title = paste ( " Box Plot of " , target , " by " , feature ) , x = feature , y = target ) } else { p <- NULL }

if ( ! is . null ( p ) ) { plots [[ i ]] <- p } }

# Arrange plots in a grid do . call ( grid . arrange , c ( plots , ncol = num _ cols ) )

# Convert categorical variables into dummy variables to include individual categories as variables . Please make sure that you do this before constructing the correlation heatmap . This would help you identify the most important features to be included in your models . It is also important to do this before training the models . X <- as . data . frame ( model . matrix ( ~ . , data = X ) ) [ , -1]

# Let ' s plot the correlation matrix heatmap to check which variables are important ( i . e . correlated with the target variable ) . # Combine X and y into one data frame data2 <- cbind (X , data . frame ( Total _ Spend = y ) )

# Convert non - numeric columns to numeric ( if applicable ) data2 <- data . frame ( lapply ( data2 , function ( x ) { if ( is . factor ( x ) ) as . numeric ( as . factor ( x ) ) else if ( is . character ( x ) ) as . numeric ( as . factor ( x ) ) else x }) )

# Compute the correlation matrix corr _ matrix <- cor ( data2 )

# Plot the heatmap corrplot ( corr _ matrix , method = " color " , tl . col = " black " , tl . cex = 0.8 , number . cex = 0.7 , addCoef . col = " black " , col = colorRampPalette ( c ( " blue " , " white " , " red " ) ) (200) , title = " Correlation Matrix Heatmap " , mar = c (0 , 0 , 1 , 0) )

# Further feature selection based on the results of pair plots and / or correlation matrix heatmap . # You can exclude variables that are not correlated with the targe variable . # When 2 variables ( other than the target variable ) are highly correlated to each other , you can remove one of them to address multi - colinearity problem ( keep the one that is highly correlated with the target variable ) . X <- data _ selected <- X [ , ! ( names ( X ) % in % c ( " Satisfaction _ Score " , " Promotion _ Response _ Responded " ) ) ]

6 Split the data into training and testing sets # Split the data into training and testing sets set . seed (123) # For reproducibility n <- nrow ( X ) splitIndex <- sample (1: n , size = round (0.8 * n ) , replace = FALSE ) X _ train <- X [ splitIndex ,] y _ train <- y [ splitIndex ] X _ test <- X [ - splitIndex ,] y _ test <- y [ - splitIndex ]

7 Train and test a Linear Regression Model # Train a linear regression model on the training data model <- lm ( y _ train ~ . , data = X _ train )

# Make predictions on the testing data predictions <- predict ( model , newdata = X _ test )

# Calculate Mean Absolute Error ( MAE ) mae <- mean ( abs ( predictions - y _ test ) )

# Calculate Root Mean Squared Error ( RMSE ) rmse <- sqrt ( mean (( predictions - y _ test ) ^2) )

# Calculate R - squared r _ squared <- 1 - ( sum (( predictions - y _ test ) ^2) / sum (( mean ( y _ train ) - y _ test ) ^2) )

# Print evaluation metrics cat ( " Mean Absolute Error ( MAE ) : " , mae , " \ n " ) cat ( " Root Mean Squared Error ( RMSE ) : " , rmse , " \ n " ) cat ( "R - squared : " , r _ squared , " \ n " )

# Plot actual vs predicted values plot ( y _ test , predictions , xlab = " Actual Total _ Spend " , ylab = " Predicted Total _ Spend " , main = " Actual vs Predicted Total _ Spend " ) abline (0 , 1)

# Display feature coefficients summary ( model )

Mean Absolute Error (MAE): 0.833809 Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE): 0.9761454 R-squared: -0.0327564

Call: lm(formula = y_train ~ ., data = X_train)

Residuals: Min 1Q Median 3Q Max -2.03876 -0.84999 -0.07502 0.84608 2.10486

Coefficients: Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|) (Intercept) -0.0003834 0.1071399 -0.004 0.9971 Age -0.0158291 0.0435626 -0.363 0.7165 GenderMale -0.1233376 0.0865233 -1.425 0.1546 Annual_Income -0.0191889 0.0433609 -0.443 0.6583 Years_as_Customer 0.1112475 0.0437163 2.545 0.0112 * Num_of_Purchases 0.0809749 0.0432673 1.872 0.0618 . Num_of_Returns 0.0308024 0.0435041 0.708 0.4792 Num_of_Support_Contacts -0.0797750 0.0432334 -1.845 0.0656 . Last_Purchase_Days_Ago -0.0586198 0.0434958 -1.348 0.1783 Email_Opt_InTRUE 0.1108428 0.0862365 1.285 0.1992 Promotion_ResponseResponded -0.0995548 0.1066615 -0.933 0.3511 Promotion_ResponseUnsubscribed -0.0897736 0.1065356 -0.843 0.3998 ChurnedTRUE 0.0922078 0.0869382 1.061 0.2894 --- Signif. codes: 0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

Residual standard error: 0.9999 on 528 degrees of freedom Multiple R-squared: 0.04138, Adjusted R-squared: 0.0196 F-statistic: 1.899 on 12 and 528 DF, p-value: 0.03204

To interpret the results (performance of the model and feature importance), please refer to the PDF “Answers to Week 3 Hands-On Exercise 2 Linear Regression - R” provided on Moodle.

8 Train a Decision Tree Regression Model # Train a decision tree model on the training data with a continuous target variable model <- rpart ( y _ train ~ . , data = X _ train , method = " anova " )

# Make predictions on the testing data y _ pred <- predict ( model , newdata = X _ test )

# Calculate regression metrics # Calculate Mean Squared Error ( MSE ) mse <- mean (( predictions - y _ test ) ^2)

# Calculate Mean Absolute Error ( MAE ) mae <- mean ( abs ( predictions - y _ test ) ) # Calculate R - squared r2 <- 1 - ( sum (( y _ test - predictions ) ^2) / sum (( y _ test - mean ( y _ test ) ) ^2) )

message ( " Mean Squared Error : " , mse ) message ( " Mean Absolute Error : " , mae ) message ( "R - squared : " , r2 )

# Plot the decision tree rpart . plot ( model )

# Display the decision tree structure in text form message ( " Tree Structure : " ) print ( model )

# Display the feature importance scores importance <- model $ variable . importance message ( " Feature Importance Scores : " ) print ( importance )

7) Num_of_Purchases>=0.8108758 124 111.693500 0.22602280 14) Num_of_Purchases>=1.158445 86 85.995880 0.09867998 28) Last_Purchase_Days_Ago< -1.245116 10 7.517683 -0.66119120 * 29) Last_Purchase_Days_Ago>=-1.245116 76 71.944410 0.19866300 58) Annual_Income>=-1.435796 69 63.650210 0.11080190 * 59) Annual_Income< -1.435796 7 2.511133 1.06472300 * 15) Num_of_Purchases< 1.158445 38 21.146890 0.51421960 * Feature Importance Scores:

Last_Purchase_Days_Ago Annual_Income 26.6646431 13.3882124 Years_as_Customer Num_of_Purchases 11.4702397 10.7907406 Promotion_ResponseResponded Age 6.0584308 5.7733313 Num_of_Returns Promotion_ResponseUnsubscribed 5.6047019 0.4931281

To interpret the results (performance of the model and feature importance), please refer to the PDF “Answers to Week 4 Further Hands-On Practice - Decision Tree in R” and “Answers to Week 3 Hands-On Exercise 2 Linear Regression - R” provided on Moodle.

9 Train a Neural Network Model # Define the model model <- keras _ model _ sequential () % >% layer _ dense ( units = 12 , input _ shape = ncol ( X _ train ) , activation = ' relu ') % >% layer _ dense ( units = 6 , activation = ' relu ') % >% layer _ dense ( units = 1 , activation = ' linear ')

# Compile the model model % >% compile ( loss = ' mean _ squared _ error ' , optimizer = ' adam ' , metrics = c ( ' mean _ absolute _ error ') )

# Train the model model % >% fit ( as . matrix ( X _ train ) , y _ train , epochs = 5 , batch _ size = 200)

# Evaluate the model score <- model % >% evaluate ( as . matrix ( X _ test ) , y _ test , verbose = 0) score [ " mean _ absolute _ error " ]

# Make predictions with the model predictions <- model % >% predict ( as . matrix ( X _ test ) )

# Print summary of predictions summary ( predictions )

# Calculate performance metrics : Mean Absolute Error , Mean Squared Error , and R - squared mae <- mean ( abs ( predictions - y _ test ) ) mse <- mean (( predictions - y _ test ) ^2) rsq <- 1 - ( sum (( y _ test - predictions ) ^2) / sum (( y _ test - mean ( y _ test ) ) ^2) )

cat ( ' Mean Absolute Error : ' , mae , '\ n ') cat ( ' Mean Squared Error : ' , mse , '\ n ') cat ( 'R - squared : ' , rsq , '\ n ')

# Evaluate feature importance using Permutation Importance # Define a prediction function for DALEX predict _ function <- function ( model , newdata ) { as . numeric ( predict ( model , as . matrix ( newdata ) ) ) }

# Create an explainer object for DALEX explainer <- DALEX :: explain ( model , data = as . data . frame ( X _ test ) , y = as . numeric ( y _ test ) , predict _ function = predict _ function , label = " Neural Network " )

# Calculate feature importances importance <- DALEX :: model _ parts ( explainer )

# Print feature importances print ( importance )

To interpret the results (performance of the model and feature importance), please refer to the PDF “Answers to Week 3 Hands-On Deep Dive - Deep Learning in R” provided on Moodle.
